perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, VIP is engaged in the business of providing Electronic Data Processing services ("EDP Services") and related consultation and services to insurance companies pursuant to computer software systems developed and owned by VIP , (the "VIP System");
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, VIP is engaged in the business of providing Electronic Data Processing services ("IT Services") and related consultation and services to insurance companies pursuant to computer software systems developed and owned by VIP , (the "VIP System");
perturbation.explanation: The term 'EDP Services' is changed to 'IT Services'. While seemingly similar, the contract relies heavily on 'EDP Services' as a defined term. Changing it creates ambiguity. The subsequent use of 'EDP Services' throughout the contract now contradicts this definition, as 'IT Services' encompasses a broader range of technological services that goes beyond the original scope defined for 'EDP Services'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: WHEREAS, VIP is engaged in the business of providing Electronic Data Processing services ("IT Services") and related consultation and services to insurance companies pursuant to computer software systems developed and owned by VIP , (the "VIP System"); WHEREAS, BNL is an insurance company domiciled in the State of Arkansas and licensed to do business in numerous additional states; and WHEREAS, VIP desires to provide EDP Services to BNL; and WHEREAS, BNL desires to obtain EDP services from VIP for the processing and administration of its insurance policies;
perturbation.justification: The change from 'EDP Services' to 'IT Services' in the initial definition directly contradicts the later use of 'EDP Services' in the 'WHEREAS' clauses. While 'IT Services' might encompass 'EDP Services', the contract specifically defines and relies on 'EDP Services' later. By changing the initial definition, the contract introduces ambiguity and inconsistency as to which services are being referred to in the contradicted text. The change creates a direct conflict as the initial definition of the service now clashes with the definition and scope of service defined later on. This misalignment makes the contract confusing, meeting the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: BNL at its expense shall obtain, install, maintain and upgrade as necessary any and all hardware, software, data and telephone lines, other communications equipment and any other equipment (hereinafter collectively referred to as the "Equipment") which it determines is necessary to allow it to use and access the VIP System pursuant to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: BNL at its expense shall obtain, install, maintain and upgrade as necessary any and all hardware, software, data and telephone lines, other communications equipment and any other equipment (hereinafter collectively referred to as the "BNL Infrastructure") which it determines is necessary to allow it to use and access the VIP System pursuant to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the defined term 'Equipment' to 'BNL Infrastructure' creates confusion. Later sections refer to 'Equipment' in contexts where it's unclear whether they now mean exclusively BNL's equipment, or potentially VIP's as well. This is particularly problematic in sections dealing with maintenance and liability.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3 .SCHEDULED AND UNSCHEDULED DOWN TIME. BNL acknowledges that there will be scheduled downtime for the routine preventive maintenance of VIP's System performed by either VIP or its vendors. VIP shall give BNL reasonable advance notice of all such scheduled downtime. BNL further acknowledges that there will also be unscheduled down-time that might occur as a result of electrical power failures and equipment failures and other acts outside of the control of VIP as contemplated in paragraph 16(J). In the event that any such down-time extends for more than two (2) consecutive working days, VIP, at its expense, will make available to BNL access to a backup facility designated by VIP for the continued processing of BNL's business. To ensure that a backup facility will be available in E - 5 case of such a failure, VIP will maintain disaster and/or business interruption insurance adequate to establish alternate site processing, as provided for in paragraph 12(A) of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change redefines 'Equipment' to 'BNL Infrastructure'. The contradicted text refers to 'equipment failures' in the context of VIP's System downtime. If 'Equipment' now exclusively means 'BNL Infrastructure', it creates ambiguity and a potential contradiction. It's unclear whether VIP is responsible for downtime caused by failures of BNL's equipment, which was not the original intent of the contract. This contradiction impacts the allocation of responsibility for downtime and the applicability of VIP's obligation to provide a backup facility, creating a strong in-text contradiction based on misaligned terminology.

perturbation.explanation: Changing "reasonable time" to "commercially reasonable time" introduces ambiguity because "commercially reasonable time" is not defined and subject to interpretation, also because BNL's failure to report to VIP in writing an error within a commercially reasonable time after BNL discovers or reasonably should have discovered such error, may also result to VIP to be not liable due to this definition change.
perturbation.location: 9. VIP REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES: DISCLAIMER: LIMITATIONS.
perturbation.contradicted_location: 9. VIP REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES: DISCLAIMER: LIMITATIONS.
perturbation.justification: While the change from "reasonable time" to "commercially reasonable time" introduces a degree of ambiguity, it doesn't create a direct, strong contradiction. Both the original and changed text in conjunction with the contradicted text express the same idea, that a failure to report an error by BNL within a certain timeframe will result in VIP not being liable. The standard to which the timeframe is being measured has changed, but is still valid, and does not contradict the idea of reporting in a timely fashion.
